rulings.Case Title: SHELDON T FONG VS. MICHAEL VRIHEAS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Compelling Responses To Form Interrogatories And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT CECILIA VRIHEAS' Motion For An Order Compelling Responses To Form Interrogatories And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Responses to the Form Interrogatories appear not to have been served by Plaintiff. Plaintiff shall provide such responses without objections by May 10, 2019. Sanctions in the amount of $100 awarded to Defendants. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: N&A MEDICAL SUPPLY, INC. VS. SAN BRUNO SKILLED NURSING HOSPITAL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To Jp Morgan Chase Bank, Na
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT JAMES PREIMESBERGER's Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To Jp Morgan Chase Bank, Na. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. JPT McDonald discloses that he and counsel for Plaintiff served together on the USF Law School Board of Governors several years ago. JPT McDonald believes that this will not affect his ability to be fair and impartial in deciding this case. While the Court requests argument on the various issues, its initial view is that the subpoena is overbroad and could encompass Mr. Preimesberger's financial privacy rights especially since the alter ego theory remains in dispute. The question remains whether the subpoena can be narrowed such as to balance Plaintiff's right to discovery versus Mr. Preimesberger's right to privacy. rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER FLOOD VS. DUNNE ENRIGHT CORP. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTs EDNA QUIGLEY, SEAN MURPHY, and DANIEL WALSH's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants Edna Quigley, Sean Murphy and Daniel Walsh's demurrer to plaintiff Christopher Flood's complaint is SUSTAINED as to both causes of action without leave to amend. As to the fiduciary breach claim, defendants owed no fiduciary duty to plaintiff as a matter of law. As pled, Buyer LLC was a manager-managed limited liability company, and members of such an LLC owe one another no fiduciary duty by reason of being members. (Cmplt. Exh. A §5.1; Corp. Code §17704.09(f)(3).) Only its manager owes duties of loyalty and care to a member of a manager-managed LLC. (Id. at (f)(1); Feresi v. The Livery, LLC (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 419, 425.) Plaintiff's argument that majority-owner members of an LLC owe fiduciary duties to minority-owner members fails for two reasons, each independently dispositive. First, plaintiff's complaint shows that the demurring defendants were not members of Buyer LLC. (Id. at Ex A §2.16.) Second, as already noted, the plain language of the Revised Uniform Limited Liability Company Act (RULLCA) says no fiduciary duty exists between members of a manager-managed LLC. (Corp. Code §17704.09(f)(3).) Jones v. Ahmanson (1969) 1 Cal.3d 93 reflects common law that pre-dated the RULLCA and does not regard an LLC in any event. Plaintiff cites no case holding that the RULLCA gives minority-owner members rights to sue majority owners for breach of fiduciary duty in a manager-managed LLC. And again, the RULLCA is to the contrary. The fraudulent concealment claim also fails. For the reasons set out above, defendants owed plaintiff no fiduciary duty, which means there was no duty to disclose. (Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum Commission v. Insomniac, Inc. (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 803, 831.) While many of them appear well-made, defendants' other points need not be reached. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court finds that the "What's App" messages are discoverable and relevant to the issues in the lawsuit. On the current record, it's impossible for the Court to determine whether the extensive redactions that have been made are appropriate. For instance, redactions occur in the middle of a discussion relating to Plaintiff's employment with Defendants. The Court requests oral argument focus on these issues but is inclined to either order that all messages be produced under the protective order or that some form of in-camera review (perhaps by a Discovery Referee) occur to evaluate any privacy claims. rulings.Case Title: ROBERT D. CAIRO VS. SAFEWAY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication, By Defendant Safeway Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT SAFEWAY, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendant Safeway, Inc. moves for summary judgment/adjudication in this case involving plaintiff's slip on a puddle in its grocery store (premises liability). The puddle and the slip are undisputed (UMF 2-6), but Safeway says it lacked actual or constructive notice of the puddle. Safeway fails to shift the burden of production on the actual or constructive notice issues. The only evidence that Safeway adduces is plaintiff's deposition testimony and there was no comprehensive discovery regarding the notice issue. The burden does not shift because plaintiff lacks personal knowledge about how long the puddle was in existence or Safeway's actual notice. (See Villa v. McFerren (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 733, 748-749 [plaintiff's factually devoid deposition testimony regarding defendant's conspiracy did not shift the burden because plaintiff lacked personal knowledge regarding that issue]; Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2007) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433 [deposition in which plaintiff machinist conceded he did not recall manufacturer's name or whether he had worked with any product bearing that name was insufficient to shift burden to survivor to demonstrate a triable issue of fact re asbestos exposure/causation].). In addition, there is a triable issue of material fact whether Safeway reasonably inspected the area where the accident occurred and/or should have had constructive notice of the puddle. Safeway's employee (Sosa) performed a three-second inspection of the area where the accident occurred immediately prior to the incident. (Sosa Depo., 21:17-23:12; Chu Dec., Ex. A.) A reasonable trier of fact could conclude that she performed a negligent inspection. (See Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 1200, 1211.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MASHA LEVINSON ET AL VS. BRIAN P. MASCHLER ESQ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANTs GORDON & REES SCULLY MANSUKHANI, LLP, BRIAN MASCHLER, and RYAN POLK's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants Gordon & Rees Scully Mansukhani LLP, Brian Maschler and Ryan Polk's demurrer to the second amended complaint is OVERRULED. Defendants rely on CCP §340.6's one-year statute of limitations. But they must establish from the complaint's face and the judicially-noticed materials that the complaint is "necessarily barred." (Favila v. Kattan Muchin Rosenman LLP (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 189, 224) (defendant's burden; "statute of limitations will not lie where the action may be, but is not necessarily barred").) Defendants' main argument is that plaintiffs learned defendants had not secured return of certain funds more than a year before suing them for malpractice, and thus "discovered" defendants' "wrongful act or omission" outside the limitations period. (See CCP §340.6(a).) Plaintiffs do not seem to dispute the bare fact that they learned the funds were not returning, but plead that defendants misrepresented the reasons why, blaming court error rather than admitting defendants' own shortcomings. (SAC 46-47, 54, 59.) Plaintiffs are lay people; indeed, they filed their initial complaint pro per. Whether plaintiffs discovered defendants' purported wrongful acts or omissions outside the limitations period raises issues more susceptible to summary adjudication than demurrer. (See Krusesky v. Baugh (1982) 138 Cal.App.3d 562, 567-68)(what constitutes malpractice notice "turns on the facts of each case").) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: WEBCOR/OBAYASHI JOINT VENTURE VS. TRANSBAY JOINT POWERS AUTHORITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-18-564434, CGC-18-566431 WITH CASE CGC-18-570621
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 8, PLAINTIFF WEBCOR/OBAYASHI JOUNT VENTURE's MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-18-564434, CGC-18-566431 WITH CASE CGC-18-570621 Webcor/Obayashi Joint Venture moves to consolidate three cases involving the Transbay Terminal Project filed in this court in 2018 - Nos. 570621, 566431 and 564434. (CCP §1048(a).) All three cases regard disputes over the project's construction costs. Thus adjudicating common issues in one court department would be more efficient than having three departments consider overlapping facts and risk inconsistent findings. (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) §12:359 ("Edmon & Karnow").) Two subcontractors oppose consolidation, but their arguments are unavailing. (1) CCP §1048(a) does not require that every issue and every party in consolidated cases be identical. (2) "There is no deadline for ordering consolidation; indeed, cases may even be consolidated on the eve of trial." (Edmon & Karnow at §12:357.) And (3) the speculative private prejudice the two subcontractors claim is outweighed by the public interest in efficient courts. The consolidation motion is granted; No. 570621 is the lead case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Webcor/Obayashi is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MEGHAN MURPHY VS. TWITTER, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 19, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF MEGHAN MURPHY's MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE GRANT. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
